data.title: Existing Home Sales Rise 1.5% To 7-Month High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales rose 1.5% in September to their highest level in seven months. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), existing home sales were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.06 million units, as expected. Existing home sales are up 4.1% compared to one year ago.

"As anticipated, falling mortgage rates are lifting home sales," said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. "Improving housing affordability is also contributing to the increase in sales."

"Inventory is matching a five-year high, though it remains below pre-COVID levels," Yun added. "Many homeowners are financially comfortable, resulting in very few distressed properties and forced sales. Home prices continue to rise in most parts of the country, further contributing to overall household wealth."

(Press release)

Background on Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes. It makes up a huge part of the residential real estate market, as 90% of purchased homes are previously owned according to the National Association of Realtors.

More importantly, there exists a strong correlation between purchases of existing homes and consumer spending. An increase in existing home sales can indirectly stimulate economic activity with increased consumer spending on new furnishings and appliances. Alternatively, a sustained drop in existing home sales often foreshadows a downturn in the economy.

Here is a snapshot of the data series, which comes from the National Association of Realtors. The data since January 1999 was previously available in the St. Louis Fed's FRED repository and is now only available for the last twelve months.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units and as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging from 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Now let's examine the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show a 23.3% increase in the US population since the turn of the century. The snapshot below is an overlay of the NAR's annualized estimates with a population-adjusted version.

Existing home sales are 22.4% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate. The population-adjusted version is 36.3% below the turn-of-the-century sales.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage in September 2025 was 6.35%, the lowest level in a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for existing homes fell for a third straight month in August, coming in at $415,200. This represents a 1.7% decline from the previous month and a 2.1% increase from one year ago, marking the 27th consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

data.title: Zegona Communications: Why I Have Been Trimming
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alluvial Fund Third Quarter 2025 Letter To Partners

Our largest position continues to be Zegona Communications (OTC:ZEGLF) and my, what a year the company has had! In August, Zegona announced a binding agreement to sell a portion of its fiber optic network joint venture to Singapore's sovereign wealth fund. When completed this quarter, the transaction will result in a large cash inflow for Zegona, enabling it to distribute a meaningful special dividend and effectively redeem the preferred shares it issued to Vodafone (VOD) in the Vodafone Spain buyout. Zegona continues to work toward monetizing another fiber optic joint venture, which should result in another large cash inflow. Together, these transactions substantially derisk the Zegona story. When we first invested, we were buying into a leveraged buyout situation where value creation was dependent on deleveraging through asset sales. Though I considered it unlikely, the process could have been derailed by skeptical regulators, jittery capital markets, or any number of macroeconomic troubles. Management has come through for shareholders in a big way with these asset sales. These transactions crystallize the value embedded in Zegona's fiber optic network, normalizing the balance sheet and allowing Zegona to focus on operational improvements.

"Multiple arbitrage" is a well-known corporate strategy where a company trading at, say, 10x earnings attempts to buy companies for 5-8x earnings, hoping the market will continue to capitalize the resulting combined earnings at 10x. Instant value creation! Happily, it also works in reverse. The market values Zegona at about 6x cash flow, but the company keeps on finding assets it can sell for 12x or more. Zegona is rumored to be considering a sale of its mobile network infrastructure in conjunction with competitor MasOrange, and may also be considering a sale of data centers it acquired with Vodafone Spain. Either transaction would result in material additional cash proceeds that could be used for deleveraging, investment in growth and efficiencies, or returned to shareholders.

Despite their excellent performance year-to-date, I continue to see upside of 50% or more in Zegona shares. The company's efforts to return to subscriber growth and improve margins should bear fruit in 2026, and further asset sales and return of capital announcements are potential catalysts.

I have been trimming our Zegona position, simply for the purposes of risk control. I think a lot about what I term "asteroid risk," the possibility that some completely unforeseeable negative event, such as an asteroid obliterating company headquarters, could cause an investment to permanently impaired. My job as portfolio manager is to ensure Alluvial Fund is never so exposed to a particular security that an unforeseeable event, be it celestial or biological in origin, could leave a smoking crater in our portfolio. Sometimes this means selling shares of fundamentally sound companies simply because they have grown to a size that exceeds sensible risk limits.

Disclosures

Investment in Alluvial Fund are subject to risk, including the risk of permanent loss. Alluvial Fund's strategy may experience greater volatility and drawdowns than market indexes. An investment in Alluvial Fund is not intended to be a complete investment program and is not intended for short-term investment. Before investing, potential limited partners should carefully evaluate their financial situation and their ability to tolerate volatility. Alluvial Capital Management, LLC believes the figures, calculations and statistics included in this letter to be correct but provides no warranty against errors in calculation or transcription. Alluvial Capital Management, LLC is a Registered Investment Advisor. This communication does not constitute a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any investment securities.

Performance Notes

Net performance figures are for a typical limited partner under the standard fee arrangement. Returns for partners' capital accounts may vary depending on individual fee arrangements. Alluvial Fund, LP has a fiscal year end of December 31, 2024 and is subject to an annual audit by Cohen & Company. Performance figures for year-to-date periods are calculated by NAV Consulting, Inc. Year-to-date figures are unaudited and are subject to change. Gross performance figures are reported net of all partnership expenses. Net performance figures for Alluvial Fund, LP are reported net of all partnership expenses, management fees, and performance incentive fees.

Contact

Alluvial welcomes inquiries from clients and potential clients. Please visit our website at alluvialcapital.com , or contact Dave Waters at info@alluvialcapital.com or (412) 368-2321.

data.title: 7 Stocks Taking The Next Step To Be On Offense
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Longleaf Partners Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Bio-Rad - Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO), a healthcare products manufacturer, was a positive contributor after the company reported steadier earnings than the market expected in a difficult environment. This sets the company up for the ability to grow earnings in 2026 and beyond, more in-line with previous industry trends. The company's important investment in Sartorius (OTCPK:SARTF) (OTCPK:SARTF) has also shown encouraging year-over-year trends following a difficult couple of years, and we are getting closer to a period when both companies will have increased strategic flexibility. In the meantime, Bio-Rad continues to be a material share repurchaser with its nearly net cash balance sheet.

PVH (PVH) - Branded apparel company PVH performed well in the quarter as the company continued to demonstrate that while quarterly earnings could have some volatility, the long-run earnings per share power of this company remains intact at over $10 per share. After hiccups at the Calvin Klein brand earlier this year, the company has gotten it back on track. PVH also remains our largest share repurchaser for the year after buying a teens percentage of shares outstanding at great prices. While this is a company that is sensitive to consumer trends, its brands resonate with a more price conscious buyer, plus the company still has numerous levers to pull to improve margins.

Rayonier (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) - We initiated positions in both Rayonier and PotlatchDeltic in the quarter. Rayonier was an immediate contributor. We discuss it more above in combination with PotlatchDeltic, as the two companies announced an accretive merger after quarter end. Southeastern has a strong long-term history investing in timberland assets, including these two companies multiple times before. This has been a tough year for housing-related stocks, and these companies have been no exception. That said, both have been on offense with accretive share repurchase and asset sales to capture the wide gap between private and public market valuations. Now they are taking the next step to be on offense by joining forces. We expect the combined company to be our largest position, which is exciting given its combination of Business, People and Price.

Albertsons (ACI) - We wrote about supermarket operator Albertsons earlier this year as a contributor in tougher stock market times. We took some off the table then but added back to our position in the third quarter on weakness. The stock was down on a quarterly earnings report that did not excite the market but was within our expectations. We were further pleased to see meaningful share repurchase at a high single digit annualized pace of shares outstanding. Later in the quarter, Albertsons' industry was pressured by news of Amazon's (AMZN) increased efforts in groceries. While we will never dismiss Amazon, we would also point out that this latest push is more of an incremental, expected move than something like the surprising acquisition of Whole Foods. This remains a competitive industry, but it is also a steadily necessary one in which Albertsons has a strong position thanks to its great real estate, brand recognition and owner alignment. Our confidence was bolstered further after quarter end when the company reported another solid set of results and announced a sizeable accelerated share repurchase, further growing value per share.

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS) - Financial software provider FIS was a detractor for the quarter. The Capital Markets segment grew 5%, which was less than expectations. Management cited volumes rebounding in Q3, but the market is skeptical. Margins were also slightly lower than expected due to an acquisition with lower margins that should revert to higher margins as it is integrated. We believe FIS' underlying fundamentals remain on-track, and the upcoming purchase of Global Payment's Issuer Solutions business and sale of FIS' remaining Worldpay stake will further improve FIS' economic predictability and increase FCF conversion. In the interim, management continues to repurchase stock and recently increased the 2025 repurchase plan by $100 million to $1.3 billion citing "the share price is just too low. So, we see it as a value."

Mattel (MAT) - Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a detractor for the quarter. Second quarter sales were below expectations as North American retailers adjusted ordering to domestic shipping vs. importing directly. This was to buy time to see if tariff rates abated, which resulted in a two-month lag in sales recognition. This resulted in North American sales down 16% while international segment sales increased 7%. Point-of-sale sell through at retailers was positive for Q2 and YTD showing healthy underlying demand as toys are somewhat non-discretionary. Mattel continues to execute operationally with improved gross margins even with lower-than-expected sales. While we believe 2025 will show overall modest sales growth, 2026 should accelerate given two new movies and at least one new digital game being launched in addition to a solid partner movie slate where Mattel produces related toys. In the interim, management is using almost all its FCF to repurchase shares at depressed prices. They plan to repurchase $340 million in 2H25 which equates to over 5% of shares outstanding.

See following page for important disclosures.

Before investing in any Longleaf Partners Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For a current Prospectus and Summary Prospectus, which contain this and other important information, visit Longleaf# . Please read the Prospectus and Summary Prospectus carefully before investing.

RISKS

The Longleaf Partners Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Mid-cap stocks held by the Fund may be more volatile than those of larger companies.

The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index is an index of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity and industry grouping, among other factors. The S&P is designed to be a leading indicator of U.S. equities and is meant to reflect the risk/return characteristics of the large cap universe. S&P 500 Value Index constituents are drawn from the S&P 500 and are based on three factors: the ratios of book value, earnings, and sales to price. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

The Russell 1000 Index measures the performance of the 1,000 largest companies in the Russell 3000 Index, which represents approximately 90% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 1000 Value index is drawn from the constituents of the Russell 1000 based on book-to-price (B/P) ratio. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a Fund and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

EBITDA is a company's earnings before interest, taxes depreciation and amortization.

The Magnificent 7 stocks are a group of large-cap companies (corporations with large market capitalizations determined by the number of shares times each share's value) in the technology sector, including Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) (parent company of Google (GOOG, GOOGL) (GOOG, GOOGL)), Amazon, Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META) (parent company of Facebook and Instagram), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA). Due to their size and performance, these stocks accounted for roughly one-third of the S&P 500's total market capitalization at the end of 2024.

As of September 30, 2025, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Fund: CNX Resources (CNX), 6.2%; Mattel, 5.8%; Kraft Heinz (KHC), 5.8%, IAC (IAC), 5.6%; EXOR, 5.6%; FedEx (FDX), 5.5%; Albertsons, 5.3%: Undisclosed, 5.2%; Regeneron (REGN), 4.9% and PVH, 4.8%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

data.title: Cloudflare: Growth Will Disappoint Long Term
data.description:
Thesis

Cloudflare, Inc. (NYSE:NET) is a perennially overvalued stock trading at nearly 40x sales and growing 27%. Management has convinced investors that Cloudflare will become the next iconic tech company, avoiding facing the fact that Cloudflare's markets, such as SASE, are highly competitive and saturated. Management has boosted Workers' potential, however, through simple logic, even if all public cloud workloads moved to Workers, Cloudflare would still not be able to justify its valuation. Finally, Workers will be a big AI loser given the lightweight workload nature of the platform. Applying a generous 35x FCF multiple on Cloudflare's normalized earnings, I estimate Cloudflare is worth 80% less today.

An Inevitable Winner

Undoubtedly, Cloudflare’s phenotype is one of extreme promise and upside. It’s hard to study Cloudflare and not get a sense of awe and excitement. And justifiably so. Here is a company with impenetrable emerging competitive advantages, visionary management, and an unprecedented first principles approach that’s on its way to becoming 1) the Internet itself, and 2) the largest computer in the world.

It truly is hard to find anything negative about Cloudflare (except the multiple). That’s because of two reasons. First, Cloudflare is an extremely technical company, particularly when it comes to Workers, and investors are extremely non-technical. As a result, it’s quite easy to hear words such as “cloud” and start dreaming of AWS. Second, Matthew Prince, Cloudflare’s CEO, has an uncanny ability to make everything seem positive and Cloudflare the undisputed heavyweight champion, before the fight even begins.

Workers TAM Exaggerated

The most exciting and, at the same time, most mysterious part of Cloudflare is Workers. Unfortunately, when investors hear cloud computing, they start dreaming of AWS, Azure, and GCP. By my analysis, and at the risk of underestimating a new technology, Workers will take significantly longer to ramp than people think, and the ceiling may be much lower than expectations.

The problem with analyzing Workers is people try and understand it bottoms-up, meaning they try and figure out what use cases you can implement. I prefer inversion. So I asked myself, let’s assume Worker captures 100% of the computing activity of the top three public clouds, what would its TAM be if you overlayed the limitation of Workers?

So we begin with the total run-rate revenue of the top three public clouds, which is approximately $250 billion for AWS by Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), earnings for Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and earnings for Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL). And now we can layer in how much of the computing activity can actually port to Workers and at what cost.

Let's assume that $250 billion worth of workloads are to move to Workers. Workers is actually 85-90% cheaper than public clouds, so $250 billion is worth closer to $25 billion worth of Workers workloads. Of course, Workers wouldn't be able to support all that because 1) Workers doesn't support long-running processes due to the limited nature of isolates, 2) Workers supports only Javascript/Typescript with only patched-up use for important languages/frameworks such as Python and Node.JS, and 3) Workers is Cloudflare-centered without any variability, and thus a ton of database/data warehouse revenue is not eligible to move. Thus, the actual TAM for Workers is much lower than $25 billion.

Of course, Workers presents an extreme lock-in for anybody that uses it because a Workers-architected application cannot run anywhere else. In addition, cloud or on-premise applications don't port automatically onto Workers, and it would take many months to years to move existing applications. Finally, it took AWS 23 years to get to $100 billion, and it had an extremely long growth runway and whitespace without competition; Workers faces tremendous competition.

Another important point is that Cloudflare is managing all its products based on the utilization % of its overall network; therefore, Workers will always have many restrictions on the usage of all its products. As a matter of fact, nearly all Workers products’ limits have remained the same since they went GA (General Availability).

Finally, although Cloudflare has put out AI products, Workers is just not suited for real AI, given that AI workloads are long-running and data-intensive processes that require farms of GPUs. Cloudflare's entire server fleet only costs ~$550 million (Page 8, 10Q), when in fact hyperscalers are spending hundreds of billions on GPUs.

I am not entirely sure what all this amounts to, but clearly, even assuming that Cloudflare obliterates all the public clouds takes away all their potential workloads, it won’t get Cloudflare more than $15 billion in revenues. Of course, it's an extreme assumption to say that Workers will steal all the workloads.

The fundamental flaw of Workers is that Cloudflare looked inward to build it rather than outward. So the thought process was, “How do we make Cloudflare more programmable so we can innovate faster?” So they built a computing platform that would cater to make Cloudflare’s very narrow use cases circa 2017 programmable (CDN, WAF, Load Balancer etc.). The problem with that is that you have created something narrow, albeit extremely novel, for the most diverse ecosystem in the world (programmers) that structurally cannot extend very much beyond its initial reach relative to the potential of a general-use platform.

An analogy would be computer processors. Think of Cloudflare as rigid graphics cards in the 1990s that eventually became programmable in the 2000s, i.e., CUDA by NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). It’s a specialized solution, that became more “general-purpose” but not nearly as general as a CPU. Whereas public clouds are just like CPUs, in that they are extremely general purpose, but don’t currently do some things as well as Cloudflare.

The question here is, will Cloudflare have its AI moment, aka a killer app of something you can only do on Cloudflare, and will it inspire millions of companies to follow? It surely hasn’t been the case for the past 8 years, but you just never know.

Don’t Trust: The SASE Story

Cloudflare will actually have a very difficult time growing its SASE business.

What most people don’t understand is that a very large majority of Cloudflare’s revenue is ACT I products today. While the company doesn’t break out revenue, there are a few indications to reverse-engineer that. It takes a little bit of speculation, but it’s probably not off.

First, sales cycles, as indicated by the company, have not changed since the company last reported it was around 80-90 days (2Q22 Earnings Transcript), and that hasn’t changed much since the company went public. Given that Zscaler, Inc. (ZS) has 12-month sales cycles, that indicates that 1) Cloudflare is selling mostly ACT I products (or ZT to small customers) and 2) Cloudflare is not selling Zero Trust to enterprises despite having 35% of F500 and >30% of Fortune 1000 as customers. And since quarterly DNR is probably ~110%, Cloudflare is most likely not expanding with ZT in these Fortune customers.

Another way to infer is by looking at cRPO. Cloudflare’s cRPO (Page 114, 10K) as % of Total RPO is 70-75%, whereas for Zscaler it is closer to 48-50%. This reflects longer contracts for Zscaler, which reinforces the idea that Cloudflare is not as present in enterprise on ZT products, since those are much more complex deployments on longer contracts.

The big implication of the above points here is that despite fancy logos on earnings calls and a perception that Cloudflare already won the SASE game, the truth is that Cloudflare is still an ACT I company. This is important because the company has been the only game in town for these types of products with limited competition.

As Cloudflare is stepping into the SASE world, this monopolistic dynamic is 100% flipped on its head. There are about 15 highly motivated, well-capitalized, and highly capable unified SASE companies. Most of these companies not only have a huge head start on Cloudflare from a revenue perspective, but they also have distribution advantages that can sustain their growth. For example, Zscaler is already selling full-blown SASE to 45% of Fortune 500 companies with $2.6bn of pure SASE revenue. Palo Alto has $1bn of SASE revenue and pretty much all the Fortune 500 companies as customers. Netskope has 25 of the F100 companies, and among them the top 4/5 largest financial F500 companies, 4/6 largest pharmaceutical F500 companies, 4/5 largest Fortune 500 telecom companies, and 2/4 largest F500 retail companies. The list goes on with Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO), Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT), The Cato Corporation (CATO), etc.

This is reinforced by Gartner’s 2024 Magic Quadrant, where Cloudflare was a “niche” player in the bottom left even after 6-7 years at it. Of course these points are static, and Cloudflare is developing fast, but it’s important to not get ahead of our skis either.

This is important because Cloudflare sports slides on investor decks about how it’s a one-stop shop, and everybody else is not. First of all, ACT I and ACT II products are sold to different departments within a company. So having an ACT I customer certainly doesn’t guarantee an ACT II win, and Cloudflare has proven that. So as a SASE player, you don’t need the ACT I products. It certainly doesn’t hurt, but it’s not what changes the game. All the top 10 SASE providers have a full set of features in their SASE portfolios.

Then, there is the question of adoption. SASE, although a cool advancement, is fundamentally a cost center and also not that much cheaper than existing hardware infrastructures. And since SASE is a bundling motion of networking/security into one provider, there is also the question of vendor lock-in, when in fact networking teams are used to stitching products from several providers. Although a large market, it’s likely that adoption will be much slower than things like social media, search, AI, e-commerce, etc.

Finally, given how saturated the market is, it’s not unlikely that this market over time becomes a race to the bottom, just like the CDN market, but of course less so. Matthew knows this and has extensively spoken about Cloudflare’s low-cost producer advantage, which is absolutely real. The problem with fixating on this advantage is that it won’t work in this industry. In an industry like oil where your margins are tiny, it pays a ton to be the lowest-cost player. This space is fundamentally different because the incremental margins on these products are sky high, and thus all players have significant room to play with.

To summarize this point, Cloudflare throws Google/Facebook/Amazon vibes on SASE when you take it at face value, but in reality, Cloudflare is actually behind, the market will see slow SASE adoption being a cost center, and there is immense competition.

Risks

My thesis has two risks. First, Workers is a new technology that attacking cloud from the bottom of the market by catering to the needs of individual developers that wouldn't otherwise have good access to cloud computing products provided by hyperscalers. According to Clay Christiansen's theory, a product like that has the potential to continue improving and climbing upmarket. Second, regarding the SASE market, Cloudflare is by far the most innovative company in the SASE market, and thus, as SASE evolves, there could be a time when the rest of the ecosystem plays catch-up with Cloudflare as opposed to the other way around.

Valuation and Conclusion

At $2 billion of run rate revenue as of 2Q25, Cloudflare's long-term model put them at 20% profit margins, or roughly $400 million. Giving this a generous 35x multiple would bring Cloudflare's equity value at $14 billion. At 348mm of outstanding shares, that's $40 per share. In other words, Cloudflare would be worth roughly 80% less than where it is right now.

Another way to appreciate how overvalued Cloudflare is would be to perform a reverse DCF to figure out what growth rates the stock price assumes, especially in the longer term. Assuming that Cloudflare generates 20% FCF margins starting this year and grows at 27% annually for the next 10 years, at a discount rate of 10%, Cloudflare's valuation would imply a perpetual growth rate of approximately 7%, which, of course, is very optimistic.

data.title: Navitas: Time To Take Profits As Two Downside Catalysts Loom
data.description:
Sometimes, being on the consensus side, even if the consensus is driven by hype, makes total sense. Certainly, this was the case for Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS), as the company has seen its share price double since my last article, when I rated it as a strong buy. Back in July, I said:

I have now doubled my exposure to this stock, which now accounts for 16% of the net liquidity value of my portfolio.

The returns of my position in Navitas have been very decent (to say the least), although I regret not betting on this stock with call options, like I'm doing right now with Canaan (I bought "worthless" OTM calls about 8% of my portfolio, up in the low triple digits by now). Notice that I used the term "betting" and not "investing," which should give you an idea of my highly speculative style.

In this piece, I explain the main reasons why I took profits in Navitas and why I believe there are two events that could lead to downside pressure on the stock. That said, I don't see asymmetric upside to short the stock, and I will explain in detail why.

Why I Believe It's Time To Take Profits And Disappear Into The Sunset

I strongly believe that the main engine powering the recent bull run was the high short interest in this stock, coupled with the shareholder optimism after the Nvidia announcement back in May this year.

Back in July, I stated:

As of July 22, the short interest of the stock is 24%, with 1.2 days to cover. To me, that's bullish, especially when considering that the short interest only began to increase after the partnership announcement with Nvidia.

Today, even after the stock price doubled, short interest is still in the low 20s.

Some bulls may think that this factor is still bullish for the stock, but I digress. And the main reason is the presence of two events on the horizon.

Q3 2025 Earnings

The first one is the next earnings release, planned for November 3, after market close. Based on my experience as a market participant, I strongly believe it could be a catalyst for profit-taking. To understand why, we have to look at the guidance that management provided in the last earnings report.

On the top line, the company expects Q3 revenue to come in at approximately $10M, plus or minus $0.5M. As noted below, in the same quarter last year, the company reported $21.7M.

If the company achieves $10M in revenue, that would imply a 54% yoy decline in the top line.

On the bottom line, management doesn't guide EPS. Instead, they guide gross margins and operating expenses. The expectation for Q3 for gross margins is 38.5% (with a tolerance of plus or minus 50 bps), and for operating expenses, $15.5M. To put this into perspective, consider that the gross margin in Q2 2024 was 40.3% (down from 41.5% in Q2 2023).

So, it seems that the decline in gross margins is coupled with the decline in the topline of the company. Not a good sign, in my view.

A very interesting metric that management guides is the share count. Here is where I believe things could turn south for investors. In the last earnings call, management guided Q3 weighted average share count to be approximately 214M shares outstanding. At the end of Q2, that number was around 199M. In my last article, I praised management's discipline to maintain a relatively stable dilution rate of around 5% per quarter (meaning, each quarter, the share count increases 5% sequentially). Specifically, I said:

The guidance for Q2 2025 implies a 5.8% yoy increase, in line with the previous trend. As for the risk of an equity financing round, the company has $75M in liquidity, and it burned $52.6M in the past 12 months. The cash runway extends to little over a year.

Well, that 214M shares outstanding guided for Q3 would represent a sequential increase close to 7.5%. That may not be a massive increase, but it may be the first hint of the equity funding ahead.

To be precise, the reason why I would take profits prior to the earnings call is that I believe management will double down on diluting shareholders. The specific catalyst that could tank shares could be, in my view, an acceleration in the dilution rate going into the fourth quarter.

Why?

Intuitively, without looking at the balance sheet, the chart below suggests dilution.

The stock is at a 4-year high (very close to the ATH from back in 2021). If there is a good moment to raise equity capital through share issuance, that moment is now.

Of course, if one dives into the balance sheet, there is a hidden surprise. A quick look at the cash and short-term investments shows a liquidity of $161.2M. That's more than double the liquidity the company had when I wrote my last article ($75M). Assuming a cash burn rate of $50M (side note: TTM operating cash outflow is $48.7M), the company has a runway of little over three years (3.2 years to be precise). I believe the company would need more cash for R&D and go-to-market spend ahead of the highly anticipated AI data-center ramps, and the most efficient way to raise that capital right now is through share issuance. Therefore, I would expect two things:

  1. Either management doesn't guide the share count for Q4 (I believe this would be a reasonable thing to do),
  2. Or, that guided share count is so off the line compared to the recent dilution rate, that it would spook investors.

Highly speculative, but in my experience dealing with hyped up stocks, this has been the case, especially in the quantum mania back in Q4 last year (and - who would have guessed - now again).

Price Action

The other catalyst that could tank the shares could be, in my view, nearing the ATH from 2021. The November 2021 high was sitting at approximately $22. Today, the share price is $17.

To be clear, there could be some room for further multiple expansion (the numerator rather than the denominator), considering the high short interest in this stock; however, I wouldn't bet on this event. That's why I took profits and walked into the sunset. I don't believe there is enough upside for a $17 to $22 move to be invested in this stock, considering the recent high volatility.

Speaking of multiples, as is the case with most hyped up stocks, Navitas doesn't have a P/E multiple (both on a TTM and FWD basis). All we have is a P/S ratio, currently trading 20x over the sector median on a 1-year forward basis.

Let me be as clear as I can, so there is no confusion among the few value-based investors who are reading this piece. In my view, the latter argument (P/S multiple) is not, in any way whatsoever, a bearish argument for this stock. It is my strongest belief as a market participant (who achieved triple-digit returns in 2024 and now in 2025, again) that valuation plays an irrelevant role for stocks during a broader bull run. When the broader markets are expanding, hyped up stocks trading at ridiculous premiums thrive, in my view, significantly more than fairly priced stocks. That's why I'm not a value investor when the broader markets are hitting all-time highs, month after month.

How Can I Be Wrong?

Well, I already said it in the last paragraph. The broader markets are still in an expansionary phase, and I am hyper bullish on the near-term outlook of the US economy, as noted by my articles on the US markets. This external factor alone could be enough to break my bear case on Navitas.

Yes, management can dilute investors. But if these investors are fine with that, and usually, during broader bull runs, this is often the case, the stock may not experience the downside movement that I predict could happen in the near term. Therefore, in no circumstance whatsoever would I consider shorting this stock. Especially at such a high short interest (22%).

From a company-wide perspective, I believe the new CEO, Chris Allexandre, could raise the levels of optimism among shareholders with updates on the GaN/SiC devices for NVIDIA's 800 VDC AI factory architecture. Earlier this month, the company released a PR announcing progress on this activity, introducing a new 100 V GaN FET portfolio optimized for lower voltage DC-DC stages on GPU power boards. Furthermore, the company emphasized the transition from legacy 54 V architectures to 800 VDC, which I believe is a key move to develop power chips for megawatt-scale AI platforms.

Furthermore, I would keep a very close eye on the recently announced Digital Power Joint Lab in Beijing. It seems that AI data centers are among the focus areas of this partnership, so I wouldn't discard the announcement of a new partnership with a chip manufacturer in this region. Such an event could bring more optimism to this stock, which will translate into double-digit moves given the high short interest.

Bottom line

I'd take profits now, but, for the reasons stated above, I wouldn't short this stock. If I still owned this stock, there are two events that I would closely track.

First, Q3 2025 earnings. Particularly, the guidance (or the lack of) for outstanding shares in Q4. Given the short cash runway, the company needs to raise capital, and considering the recent run, dilution is the way that makes the most sense, in my view.

On the flip side, if the new CEO is able to impress investors with more announcements during the next earnings call, especially if that involves a partnership with a chip maker in China, that could overshadow the dilution and likely lead to a bull run. Readers should weigh the chance of this event happening. In my view, this is a low chance scenario, which makes me rate this stock as a strong sell.

data.title: Amphenol: Another Great Quarter But Shares Are Overvalued
data.description:
Electronic components maker Amphenol (NYSE:APH) announced earnings on Wednesday. And, by all accounts, it was another tremendous report, with the company easily topping expectations and delivering another quarter of blistering growth.

That probably isn't too surprising, as Amphenol has been putting out earnings releases that have been far better than expectations all year now. While Amphenol's electronic components business might seem like a fairly unglamorous one, it has proven to be in just the right place and right time for the current boom in AI- and data center-driven spending.

One of Amphenol's long-term advantages has been its ability to seamlessly integrate a wide range of M&A deals over the years. That acquisitive nature has continued in recent months. In August, Amphenol announced the $1 billion acquisition of Trexon. Trexon provides high-reliability, interconnect, and cable lines primarily for defense industry customers. Amphenol disclosed that Trexon is forecasting EBITDA of approximately $290 million and an EBITDA margin of 26% for the current year.

Then there's the more than $10 billion deal to buy CommScope's (COMM) connectivity and cables business ("CCS"), which I already profiled. Amphenol's management stated this week that the CCS deal is ahead of schedule and is now projected to close by the end of Q1 2026.

While those M&A deals will give Amphenol a tailwind over the next year, let's dig into the Q3 numbers in particular.

Q3 Earnings: Another Fantastic Print

On Wednesday, Amphenol released its latest quarterly earnings report. Like in recent quarters, the Q3 results exceeded all expectations.

Non-GAAP EPS of 93 cents easily topped the 80-cent analyst consensus and leapt from 50 cents in the same period of last year. And revenues of $6.19 billion jumped 53% year-over-year and topped analyst expectations by more than $600 million. This was a huge beat by any measure.

The company benefited to a degree from acquisitions, such as its August 2025 purchase of Rochester Sensors, which will add roughly $100 million in annual revenues going forward. Even excluding M&A, however, organic revenues soared 41% year-over-year. Amphenol is absolutely in the right part of the industry to cash in on the AI boom.

In addition to the tremendous earnings results, Amphenol also impressed investors with an eye-catching 51% dividend increase. This hike raised the quarterly dividend payout from 16.5 cents to 25 cents per share.

This $1.00 annual dividend still represents a less than 1% dividend yield at today's stock price. However, Amphenol has grown its dividend at annualized rates of 21% and 18% over the past five and 10 years, respectively. So, while the starting yield isn't all that much to get excited about, the growth trajectory has been more than impressive.

As for dividend safety, despite the rapid increases, the dividend should be easily covered out of earnings. And, as per the latest conference call, the company's net leverage ratio was a modest 0.7x at the end of Q3. This leaves plenty of room for the company to complete its acquisition of CommScope's connectivity business while still funding these dividend increases as well.

How Long Will The Boom Last?

It seems likely that Amphenol will have a strong 2026 as well. The AI boom remains in full swing, and there do not appear to be too many signs of a near-term slowdown. That said, Amphenol is unlikely to continue growing revenue at a more than 50% annualized rate for all that long, either. That sort of growth is exceptionally difficult to maintain at a large, mature firm such as Amphenol.

And we saw perhaps the first signs of a potential slowdown in the company's Q3 results. As the company noted in its conference call:

Orders in the quarter were a record $6.111 billion, up a strong 38% compared to the third quarter of 2024 and up 11% sequentially, resulting in a book-to-bill ratio of 0.99:1.

This 38% year-over-year jump in orders is still tremendous, to be clear. However, it could represent a bit of a deceleration from the 50%+ revenue growth that the company has posted over the past two quarters. Amphenol's book-to-bill ratio also came in slightly below one, indicating that the velocity of new orders appears to be decreasing a bit compared to the size of Amphenol's existing business.

It's notable that analysts simply don't believe the current euphoric levels of CAPEX growth will last long. After Amphenol's projected 50% jump in revenues this year, analysts see that falling off to just 11% next year, followed by a similar-sized increase in 2027:

It might be tempting to blame this on stale estimates. However, Amphenol is widely covered and received 15 positive analyst earnings revisions over the past three months:

Analysts, even after factoring in all the new information and deals announced in the AI space, still seemingly believe that Amphenol is getting relatively close to a saturation point where its growth trajectory will roll over.

It's perfectly fine to think or guess that Amphenol will be able to keep up its frenetic growth rate well into 2026 or perhaps even 2027. However, it's worth noting that the analyst consensus thinks the current torrid growth phase will soon draw to a close despite all the positives we see in the industry right now.

This isn't to knock the 11% growth that analysts are forecasting for Amphenol in more normalized demand conditions, either. Double-digit topline revenue growth is excellent. But when you're paying 40x current-year earnings, even 11% topline annual increases probably aren't enough to support that valuation.

APH Stock Verdict

I get the temptation to throw valuation to the wind and buy APH stock regardless. The company is absolutely firing on all cylinders right now.

That is, however, the issue with cyclical companies. Things look absolutely the best right near the top of the cycle. It is once growth slows that valuation comes back into focus.

Amphenol has historically been a secular growth story, which is to say that it has consistently grown the business year in and year out thanks to its internal diversification, disciplined capital allocation, and skilled M&A deals. You could afford to pay up for APH stock because it would almost inevitably grow into the valuation in due time.

Now, though, I'm concerned that Amphenol has become so heavily tied to AI-related spending that its revenues will slump once the current spending boom lets up. That will be even more true once the CCS acquisition is finished and Amphenol is further linked to AI- and data center-related revenues.

Like in the fallout from the dot-com era, once fiber optic cable CAPEX collapsed, I expect a similar plunge in AI spending once we're past the peak. It used to be that Amphenol was so heavily diversified internally that weakness in any one of its end markets could be easily overcome by growth in the rest of the business. I believe that will no longer be the case once the current AI spending boom starts to subside.

This could all be unwarranted concern. Perhaps the AI spending boom will continue for many more years, and Amphenol will easily grow into its current valuation. I'm at a hold rating, not a sell, on APH stock because this company has excellent management and is executing skillfully on the present market opportunity. That said, I believe valuations will get hit at the first sign of a slowdown in AI spending.

Long story short, APH stock is now up 95% year-to-date. That's much faster than the company's growth rate. Investors are pricing in higher and higher expectations even as the AI boom may be maturing. That makes the risk/reward picture here incrementally less favorable.

I'm at a "Sell" rating on most AI-related stocks, as I believe investor enthusiasm has run far ahead of fundamental developments. That said, Amphenol is one of the few companies that is legitimately making good on the potential here. I can't endorse the valuation, but the company is performing brilliantly right now, and that's good enough to maintain a "Hold" rating for now.

data.title: HPS: Strong Historical Performance, Good Positioning, And Overpriced
data.description:
The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III (NYSE:HPS) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that aims to provide its investors with a very high level of income through investments made into fixed-income securities such as preferred stocks and bonds. Despite the name of this fund, though, it is not entirely invested in preferred stocks. According to the fund’s website, HPS only has 55.82% of its portfolio invested in preferred stocks, along with 40.14% invested in corporate bonds:

This is a somewhat different allocation than most closed-end preferred stock funds possess, as the overwhelming majority of them are invested almost entirely in preferred stocks, as befitting their names. Only the three John Hancock preferred stock funds are any different.

There could be some positives and negatives to this fund’s apparent strategy of splitting its assets between preferred stocks and corporate bonds. For example, one major advantage of corporate bonds is that investors originally receive the face value of the bond when it matures. Thus, investors in these bonds do not usually suffer any nominal losses unless the bond issuer defaults.

Of course, there is no guarantee that the bond investor will earn a positive real return, especially on long-term investment-grade bonds or U.S. Treasuries, as the yields of these securities might not be sufficient to offset the compounding impacts of inflation over an extended period. Preferred stocks, on the other hand, do not typically mature, and over time, the dividend payments of these securities will have lesser and lesser purchasing power, so they may actually end up delivering lower real returns over time than short-term bonds, depending on the relative yields of the two assets.

Fortunately, preferred stocks tend to have higher yields than most bonds issued by the same entities, which helps to offset the fact that preferred stocks do not typically have a maturity date that ensures the return of an investor’s initial capital. As of the time of writing, the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index (LQD), which tracks the price and yield performance of domestic investment-grade corporate bonds, has a trailing twelve-month yield of 4.30%. The ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF), which tracks the price and yield performance of investment-grade preferred stocks, has a trailing twelve-month yield of 6.50%, a 2.20% yield advantage. The higher yield of preferred stock may be sufficient to attract some income investors who might otherwise prefer the relative safety of corporate bonds. However, we should keep in mind that the lack of a maturity date could still result in preferred securities delivering lower real returns than short-term bonds in a high inflation environment.

Regular readers are well aware that I expect inflation to be somewhat higher than most of us are accustomed to going forward, so personally, I am choosing not to invest in preferred securities or any long-dated bonds. However, someone who expects inflationary pressures to ease (such as what might occur in a recession) may find something to like in a high-yielding fixed-income closed-end fund with a mixture of preferred stocks and corporate bonds, such as HPS.

Peer Distribution Comparison

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III boasts a substantially higher yield than most index funds tracking similar assets. This chart shows the current yields of some of the major domestic fixed-income indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.79%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.56%

Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index

4.30%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index

6.50%

The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III has an 8.59% yield at the current market price, so it is considerably higher than any of the indices shown above. It has roughly a 2.09% yield advantage over the domestic preferred stock index, which would result in investors in HPS receiving an extra $20,900 in annual yield for every $1 million investment than the preferred stock index would deliver. An investment in the junk bond index would deliver slightly more income than the preferred stock index (about $600 per year for every $1 million invested), but it is still far inferior to what HPS delivers. For an investor whose goal is to earn the maximum amount of income possible, the John Hancock fund appears to be a superior choice.

However, the fund does not have nearly as great an advantage over similar closed-end funds in terms of yield. We can see that in this yield comparison chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

8.59%

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (PSF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

7.30%

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund (FPF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

8.75%

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund (FFC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

7.04%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

9.89%

There are five funds shown in this peer comparison, with the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III representing the median fund in terms of yield. As such, we can see that the fund’s yield is far less impressive when it is compared to its peers than when it is compared to ordinary index funds. Income investors frequently try to maximize the income that they receive from the assets in their portfolios, so this could suggest that some of the peer funds may be somewhat more attractive to investors for whom that is the primary goal. However, the fact that this fund’s yield is generally in line with its peers appears to suggest that the market is not expecting a distribution cut here. Rather, it believes that the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III should be able to sustain its distribution at the current level.

The fund’s performance year-to-date appears to support the idea that its distribution is sustainable. This chart shows the performance of its net asset value since the start of this year:

As shown, the net asset value per share of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III has risen by 0.34% since the start of this year. This means that the fund has managed to fully cover all of the distributions that it has paid out solely with its investment profits. This is a good sign, but we do notice two things here. The first is that HPS barely managed to cover its distributions, as its net asset value is only up 0.34% year-to-date. Thus, any weakness in the fixed-income markets or the occurrence of an event that causes fixed-income asset prices to decline could threaten the fund’s ability to sustain its distribution. After all, such an event would push the fund’s net asset value performance into negative territory.

The second thing that we notice here is that the fund was failing to cover its payout for most of the current year. The fund’s net asset value fell below its starting point in early March, and it stayed there until the first week of September. Thus, we do have something of a precedent for poor performance of the fixed-income markets, and a similar event in the future could push the fund’s net asset value negative and threaten its distribution. There is a possible catalyst for such an event in the near term, which we will discuss later in this article.

Advantages Of The Fund’s Mixed Portfolio

The mixed portfolio of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III might have given it a share price performance advantage in recent years as well. Over the past ten years, shares of the fund have declined by 9.61%. While this is disappointing, consider that the prices of nearly all fixed-income assets declined over the period, which was largely due to the increase in interest rates that occurred in 2022 and 2023:

The major thing that we notice here is that preferred stocks underperformed bonds in terms of price performance over the period. The PFF ETF, which tracks an index of domestic preferred stocks, declined by 19.87% compared to the 7.52% decline of investment-grade bonds. HPS’s performance comes in right between the performance of the two indices, which suggests that the bonds in the fund’s portfolio boosted its performance compared to what an all-preferred stock fund would have delivered.

As we might expect from the fund’s superior yield relative to both indices, it outperformed them by quite a lot in terms of total return:

This is fairly typical for fixed-income closed-end funds. As fixed-income products deliver all of their net lifetime investment returns through the distributions that they pay out, so yield is critical for total returns over the long term with this asset class. The superior yields of nearly all closed-end funds relative to the indices result in them outperforming comparable index funds over time, in most cases.

The performance of the fund relative to its peers has also been very solid. HPS was the best-performing fund in the peer group in terms of share price performance over the past ten years:

This could very easily be due to the sizable bond allocation in HPS’s portfolio, as that bond allocation appears to have prevented the fund’s net asset value from declining as much as the other funds experience. While the share prices of closed-end funds do not always move in line with their net asset values, there is a very strong positive correlation between the two, especially over long periods of time. Thus, the positive impact of the bonds in the portfolio could have resulted in this fund’s share price holding up better than its peers.

On a total return basis, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III was also the best performer of its peer group over the trailing ten-year period:

This does not contradict the statement that I made earlier about yield being critical to the long-term performance of these funds for a few reasons. The first is that the improved share price performance of HPS relative to its peers is one reason why its yield is not as good as two of the other funds shown. As the share price of a fund rises, its yield declines. Another reason is that because HPS delivered a better share price performance over the period, a lower proportion of its distribution went to offset capital losses than was the case with the other funds.

Finally, HPS was one of the very few fixed-income funds that did not cut its distribution over the 2022 to 2024 period as rising interest rates put pressure on fixed-income asset prices. The fund’s last distribution cut was back in 2019. This undoubtedly helped its total return performance compared to a fund such as FFC, which is still paying out a smaller distribution than it did in 2023.

Overall, it does appear that we can make a case for the preferred stock/corporate bond mixed portfolio of HPS over the pure preferred stock portfolios of many of the fund’s peers. Naturally, though, the fund is far from perfect, and it is certainly possible that the performance of its peers will be better than HPS going forward. This is a real possibility if preferred stocks end up outperforming bonds. However, right now, the signs point to potential troubles with preferred stocks, in which case HPS could prove to be the best fund out of its peer group to hold.

Recent Performance Update

We previously discussed the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III on April 12, 2024. As such, approximately eighteen months have passed since our last discussion on this fund. There are a great many things that can occur in a given eighteen-month period, and the eighteen-month period in question is certainly no exception to this. In fact, the last eighteen months have been very eventful, with the leadership of the U.S. government changing (which always causes changes to the macroeconomic conditions as the incoming government seeks to implement their economic agenda) and the Federal Reserve stepping back on its monetary tightening efforts and beginning to ease conditions, so we should take a few moments to discuss these changes as they are important to understanding the performance of this fund over the period.

Perhaps the most important event to occur over the past eighteen months was the Federal Reserve’s attempts to ease monetary policy. Back in April 2024, the effective federal funds rate was 5.33%, which corresponds to a federal funds target range of 525 to 550 basis points. The central bank has since reduced the federal funds target range to 400 to 425 basis points, which gives us an effective federal funds rate of 4.11% today:

This was a total of 125 basis points of interest rate reductions, but it was done over the course of four meetings as opposed to five (the first cut was a 50-basis-point reduction). Interestingly, though, the yields on longer-term bonds have not fallen as much as may be expected from 125 basis points of interest rate reductions. We can see that in this chart:

Bond Term

Yield on April 12, 2024

Yield on September 16, 2024

Yield Today

Ten-Year

4.50%

3.63%

3.97%

Twenty-Year

4.73%

4.01%

4.52%

Thirty-Year

4.61%

3.94%

4.54%

(Yields are for U.S. Treasury securities of the term stated.)

We can see that bond yields did not decline by 125 basis points over the period. In fact, the twenty-year and thirty-year U.S. Treasury bonds saw their yields decline by 21 basis points and 7 basis points, respectively, despite 125 basis points of cuts. All three of the U.S. Treasury securities have higher yields today than they did on September 16, 2024, which was the day before the Federal Open Market Committee implemented the first 50-basis point cut.

As bond prices move inversely to bond yields, the interest rate cuts have proven not to be as bullish for bond prices as many analysts expected. One of the biggest reasons for this has almost certainly been inflation, which accelerated following the first interest rate cut in September 2024 and has been proving to be very stubborn at levels well above the official 2% target:

The last time that the official inflation rate was less than 2% was in February 2021. Despite this, the Federal Reserve is cutting interest rates as though it has won the battle against inflation. The market apparently does not believe that inflation has been beaten (and the data support this viewpoint), so bond yields remain elevated as investors try to ensure that they will earn a positive real return from the bonds that they hold.

Preferred stocks exhibit some similarities with bonds in terms of structure. As with bonds, most preferred stocks pay out a yield that is fixed at the time of issuance or pay a dividend that is based on some benchmark such as the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. The dividend of these securities does not change with the performance of the issuing company. Thus, the dividend paid by these securities is much more similar to a bond’s coupon than to the dividend paid by common equities. However, preferred stocks do not usually have a maturity date or if they do, it is many years into the future. As such, preferred stocks tend to behave more like bonds (particularly long-term bonds) in their pricing than like common stocks. Given what we see with bond yields since our last discussion on the fund, we can expect HPS to have smaller gains from the interest rate cut than may be expected.

Another major event that occurred during the past eighteen months is that the United States changed its leadership. In isolation, this would probably not have a major impact on the performance of preferred stocks or corporate bonds. However, the new leadership has been very aggressively pushing for lower interest rates and very loose financial conditions. In other words, the current administration is pushing for a return to the negative real rate era that dominated the period between the financial crisis and pandemic.

The Federal Reserve has so far resisted the political pressure, although it is cutting rates (just not as quickly as the administration is demanding), but there is no guarantee that it will continue to do this once Jerome Powell’s term ends next year. However, as we saw over the past year, cuts to the federal funds rate do not necessarily bring down long-term bond or preferred stock yields if inflation remains elevated. Thus, a continuation of the interest rate cutting policy by the Federal Reserve may not actually be beneficial for the share price or net asset value performance of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III.

Overall, we might not expect the performance of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III to be particularly impressive since our last discussion. After all, long-term interest rates have not come down as much as might be expected from the Federal Reserve’s rate cuts, and inflation still remains a problem.

This is pretty much what we see when we look at a chart of the fund’s performance:

As we can see here, preferred stocks are almost flat since our last discussion on HPS, while investment-grade bonds are up a slight 5.48%. The shares of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III are up 3.09% over the period, which is relatively flat when we consider that eighteen months passed over the period. However, we do see that the fund’s shares exhibited some volatility over the period, as they rose quite a bit between April 2024 and early October 2024, and then they declined just as quickly once the interest rate cuts started. Investors who simply bought and held their positions over the full eighteen-month period experienced little change in the value of their positions, however.

Naturally, those who actually reinvested the distributions that the fund paid out saw much better results than those who simply took the fund’s distributions as income:

As shown here, anyone who reinvested the distributions that the fund paid out over the period benefited from a 17.37% total return. This was far better than the performances of either domestic preferred stocks or domestic corporate bonds. Thus, the fund continued its long-term trend of outperforming the fixed-income indices. While the fund’s performance was not especially impressive compared to the performance of other things in the market, it did manage to do pretty well against comparable indices. Any disappointment in the performance of this fund is due to the fact that both bonds and preferred stocks delivered relatively weak performances over the period in question.

About The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III

The website for the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income while still preserving the value of its capital:

We should note that this means that the fund is trying to preserve the nominal value of its capital, not the real value. Investment-grade bonds delivered a negative total real return over the past ten years, while preferred stocks were barely positive:

These figures are the total returns (assuming investors reinvested all dividends and coupon payments) of domestic investment-grade bonds, preferred stocks, and domestic junk bonds after adjusting for inflation over the past ten years. We can clearly see that only junk bonds managed to deliver an average total real return that was more than 1% per year. Investment-grade bonds were negative, which, of course, means that investors in them actually lost purchasing power. While bonds do usually protect investors against capital losses on a nominal basis, they do not offer any sort of protection against the loss of purchasing power that comes with inflation. As such, HPS is merely attempting to protect its investors against the loss of nominal capital, not the loss of value in real terms.

As already mentioned, HPS primarily seeks to achieve its goals by investing in a portfolio of preferred stocks and bonds, although right now it is more heavily weighted towards preferred stocks. A significant proportion of these appear to be banks. The fund’s most recent annual report provides the following sector allocations for this fund:

Sector

% of Net Assets

7.0%

Consumer Discretionary

1.8%

Consumer Staples

0.2%
