8.5%

Financials

98.7%

Health Care

0.8%

Industrials

1.1%

Information Technology

1.8%

Materials

1.0%

Real Estate

3.2%

Utilities

33.2%

(Figures provided are combined totals of preferred stocks, corporate bonds, and capital preferred securities.)

Entities specifically stated to be “Banks” comprise 47% of the fund’s net assets as of July 31, 2025. This is something that might concern some investors who follow the banking sector, as fears have recently emerged regarding the quality of their loans. As the Los Angeles Times states:

Wall Street is concerned about the health of the nation’s regional banks, after a few of them wrote off bad loans to commercial customers in the last two weeks and caused investors to wonder whether their might be more bad news to come.

The Los Angeles Times also notes that James Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), stated that there may be more losses that have not yet been reported. JPMorgan Chase itself had to take a $170 million write-off in the third quarter due to problems with loans granted to Tricolor, which has been accused of obtaining loans under fraudulent pretenses. Tricolor was not the only auto-related company to collapse recently, either, as auto parts supplier First Brands also sought out bankruptcy protection in September. JPMorgan Chase can absorb a $170 million loss without too much trouble, but the concern here is that the losses may be more widespread and larger than currently stated, which could ultimately put pressure on the price of preferred stocks. In other words, there may be some who are worried about another situation like what occurred back in 2008.

Fortunately, for now, this does not seem to be a problem. As BlackRock (BLK) states in the article that I just linked to, most of the problems seem to be contained to collateralized loan obligations and private credit markets. The banks themselves do not have enough exposure to bad loans to put pressure on their ability to sustain their preferred stock dividends. Investors in CLO funds or funds that invest in leveraged loans (such as most floating-rate debt funds) might take some losses from this situation, but a fund like HPS should be fine. The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III does not have any leveraged loans or collateralized loan obligations in its portfolio, and its exposure to individual banks is fairly limited. As we can see here, the fund’s largest position is in Bank of America (BAC) at 6.48% of its total assets, followed by Wells Fargo & Company at 5.03% of its total assets:

There are only two banks that account for more than 5% of the fund’s total assets, and neither of those positions is much more than 5% of the fund’s total assets. Thus, there does not seem to be any real reason for investors in this fund to worry about suffering a default or something similar from the current problems in the credit market. However, there may be some pressure on preferred stocks until this situation is resolved, so the upside potential could be limited for a while. However, investors who would simply be purchasing this fund for income do not appear to have a reason to worry about it too much.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with that objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.11 per share ($1.32 per share annually), which gives the fund an 8.59% yield at the current share price. This fund has been fairly consistent with respect to its distribution, as the payout has remained stable since October of 2019. However, the fact that the fund’s distribution has been stable for six years does not mean that it can actually afford the distribution. As such, we should have a look at the fund’s finances in order to see how well it is covering its payout and how sustainable it actually is.

For this purpose, we will consult the fund’s annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on July 31, 2025. As this is a newer report than the one that was available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, it should work reasonably well to provide us with an update.

For the twelve-month period that ended on July 31, 2025, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III reported net investment income of $30,283,060. This was not sufficient to cover the $42,304,185 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Unfortunately, it appears that this fund failed to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on July 31, 2025, HPS reported net realized losses of $4,666,842, which were wholly offset by $30,932,549. The fund overall generated investment profits of $38,932,549 over the twelve-month period:

This was still not enough to fully cover the distribution, however. The annual report states that $8,894,649 (about 21.03% of the total distribution for the fiscal year) was a return of capital as opposed to actual investment earnings. While a return of capital can be a reasonable way to distribute money to its shareholders from an income tax perspective, it can be troublesome if it causes the fund’s net asset value to decline, as was the case during the most recent period.

Fortunately, though, if we look at the previous year, everything looks fine. Over the past two years, the fund has reported total investment profits of $104,468,311:

It only distributed $84,468,681 over the two-year period. Thus, overall, the fund has managed to cover its distributions over the past two years, even though it failed to cover in the most recent one in isolation. There does not seem to be too much to worry about right now, but investors might still want to keep an eye on the fund’s net asset value just in case things change.

Valuation

Shares of HPS are currently trading at a 6.31% premium on net asset value. This is a fairly large premium that is quite a bit higher than the 5.54% premium that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is also fairly expensive when compared to its long-term average price. Over the past five years, shares of HPS have traded at prices ranging from a 4.84% discount to a 14.25% premium on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 3.89% premium, which is expensive, but still cheaper than the price that the fund’s shares have today.

Here is how the current price of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III compares to that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III

6.31%

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund

(6.19%)

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

(5.79%)

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund

(6.29%)

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

(0.49%)

As of the time of writing, the only preferred stock funds that are trading at premiums are the ones from John Hancock. This is probably due to the relatively strong performance that these funds have delivered relative to their peers in recent years, but it is still too much to pay for them. It is very hard to make a case that paying more for fixed-income securities makes sense, regardless of the historical performance of the fund. In this case, the current premium is well above the average for HPS and as such, it makes the most sense to wait for the share price to decline somewhat before buying shares.

HPS Expense Ratio

The fund’s fact sheet states that HPS has an expense ratio of 5.14%. The annual report states that the fund’s expense ratio is 1.28% if we were to exclude the money that it has to pay in interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III is a historically very well-performing preferred stock and bond fund. The bond exposure may contribute to this fund’s outperformance relative to other preferred stock funds in recent years, as bonds held up much better than preferred stock once interest rates started to rise. Preferred stock prices might remain under pressure for a little while due to inflation apparently worsening and investors being somewhat concerned about the exposure that banks have to weaknesses in the credit markets. This fund should be able to ride through any negative effects from the loan problems, and its yield is high enough that investors do not need a lot of share price appreciation in order to earn a reasonable return.

The biggest problem with this fund right now is that it is trading at a price that is well above the actual value of its assets, so anyone buying the fund today is basically buying preferred stocks and corporate bonds at a price that is higher than they are actually worth. It is difficult to make a case for paying such a high premium for the fund’s shares.

data.title: QuantumScape: Up 180% - This Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
Investment Thesis

QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) is up 10% today, October 23rd, after yesterday's post-market earnings release. I'm not sure if the results warrant the appreciation per se, but I think, more broadly, the recent technological and strategic developments significantly reduce the risk and put QuantumScape closer to commercializing its disruptive battery tech.

The company is up more than 180% this year.

Some fellow analysts would argue that QS, with a $7.7 billion market cap and no revenue, is overvalued. I can understand that. But I think investing in QS requires a long-term vision. The recent developments lend confidence in QS's ability to execute on that vision, warranting a higher valuation. If successful, QS's share price could multiply from here.

Q3 In A Nutshell: Smaller Loss, Longer Runway

In Q3 '25, QS posted $106 million in net loss, or -18 cents per share, versus the -20 cents estimate, so, technically, they beat estimates, which is always a good thing. However, I think the story here is a reaffirmation of the capital-light business model pivot. The company guided to lower CapEx in the earnings release—in the $30–$40 million range, down from $45–$75 million prior guidance—and said that its cash runway has increased to the end of 2029, rather than 2028, thanks to the recent royalty prepayment by PowerCo (more about this below).

The company also began shipping its first B1 (QSE-5) samples using its proprietary, low-cost, high-yield Cobra method (more about this below).

Overall, these are all positive developments, but in my view, the market's reaction is a bit over the top. Having said that, I'm bullish on this ticker for the following reasons.

Cobra - A Step Towards Mass Production

Earlier this year, QS announced that it had successfully implemented its new "Cobra" manufacturing process for its ceramic separator, improving heat-treatment speed by a dramatic 25x. The ability to manufacture at scale is a very serious concern, and any advancement is welcomed, let alone a 25x improvement. We all expected QS to gradually lower production costs as commercialization ramps up, and this dramatic technological breakthrough, even before QS started commercial production, sets high expectations.

Finally, A Real-World Demo

Last month, Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) and PowerCo showcased the first vehicle powered by the QSE-5 B1 samples that QS has been shipping for the past few quarters. Shares jumped over 24% that day. I suppose it was nice to see a live, tangible demonstration of QS's efforts.

QS has been working with Volkswagen since 2012 and has been a central part of its investor pitch since then. So, seeing that Ducati reaffirmed the next stage of the partnership: real-life data collection.

Typically, the automotive verification process follows a standard scheme, with B samples often followed by a C sample, which aims to hone the product before final design approval. We're still at least a couple of years away from QS SSBs being in private vehicles.

Volkswagen Money. Less CapEx And Lower Risk

Last year, QS pivoted to a capital-light business model. That was a dramatic shift. Management signed an agreement with PowerCo, allowing the latter to use its tech to manufacture up to 40 GWh capacity. It's a joint manufacturing facility with PowerCo (part of Volkswagen), which shifts some of the execution risk and manufacturing expenses, but it comes at a cost. The manufacturing IP is now shared with PowerCo (QS keeps the IP for the ceramic electrolyte). In return, QS will collect royalties, I suppose, per kWh or per battery pouch. In any case, QS received a prepayment of these royalties—$130 million. But these are conditional on QS continuing its collaboration with PowerCo to build the joint facility and on its continued improvement of its product.

In July, the agreement was extended with an additional $130 million pre-payment, an option to extend capacity by 85 GWh, and the right of PowerCo to sell batteries to third parties.

The money is great, obviously. But I think this is more about Volkswagen showing commitment. They're putting their money where their mouth is.

Supply Chain Heavyweights Support

QS signed two deals with two materials heavyweights: Murata Manufacturing (OTCPK:MRAAY) and Corning (GLW). For a development-stage company to be acknowledged by these two companies is a big deal. Murata Manufacturing is a $35 billion heavyweight, while Corning is even larger, with a market capitalization of $72 billion. They're investing in manufacturing capacity to supply QS with its ceramic electrolyte, which, from my understanding, will then be shipped to the PowerCo joint manufacturing facility for final production.

What QS Could Be Worth

I think these developments put QS closer to commercialization. The company has an F Growth Quant score on valuation, and for a good reason. It doesn't have a commercial product yet.

Investing in QS isn't for everyone. This is a bet on the future, with high risk and high rewards.

The market size is huge. Contemporary Amperex Technology (OTC:CTATF) alone commands annual sales of over $52 billion. LG Energy' TTM sales totaled $17.5 billion. QS doesn't have to dominate the market to justify its $7.7 billion market cap. Even a fraction of the market will suffice, and with the backing of the second-largest automotive OEM and talks with other leading car manufacturers, this isn't a fantasy but a realistic scenario. A humble 5% market share, based on a 2030 lithium-ion battery market size of $200 billion, equates to $10 billion in sales. A conservative P/S ratio of 3x puts QS's valuation at $30 billion.

Final Thoughts And Risks

I think the main risk is that, while these contracts are backed by real cash, they aren't exclusive and could be terminated. Volkswagen can change its mind. With a dynamic battery tech landscape, this is a real risk. There is no consensus on which solid-state battery chemistry is better.

Moreover, traditional ion battery technology is closing the energy density gap with SSB. I recently wrote an article about Amprius, a Li-ion battery disruptor with what seem to be the best batteries on the market today, outperforming QS's in key metrics, including energy density. Amprius lists Boeing and the US military as its primary customers.

Overall, I think QuantumScape Corporation is solidifying its path towards commercialization with hard cash and non-binding commitments. Given the commercial potential, I think this is an attractive play, at least to diversify from the AI exposure.

data.title: Micron: The Most Attractive Artificial Intelligence Play
data.description:
Thesis

Micron Technology is my single biggest holding, and at a forward P/E of 11x, it's the most attractive AI play deserving of your attention. It makes the high-bandwidth memory, HBM, that powers leading-edge AI chips for both NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD). Yes both NVIDIA and AMD. Demand is so strong that its HBM is sold out through 2025, and pricing is already locked for next year. Their full year revenue was up 49% year-over-year, which came in at $37.4 billion. The solid 2025 results and the transparent 2026 financials show that Micron is no longer cyclical, nor a commodity, but a critical advanced technology supplier to the large and growing data center marketplace. While others speculate on uncertain technologies and who will be the AI winners, Micron has proven that it is an AI powerhouse, and yet it still trades at a reasonable valuation.

The market is slow to recognize Micron's value due to its long cyclical history. Investors in Micron have seen boom years in the past, and could see 2025 as just another boom year. However, the AI-driven changes are going to last for years and are fundamentally rewriting the sales and margins for Micron. As the market recognizes Micron’s value, and new business realities, a 20-30x earnings multiple seems inevitable. Even at 20x next year's earnings of around $17, that implies a stock price over $334. If it re-rates higher, we could see the $400's. Micron is one of the clearest opportunities I see in AI infrastructure and why I believe Micron is the most attractive AI play.

Growth of Stability

Historically, Micron was tied to brutal commodity pricing cycles, or what I call uninvestable. The company posted an $8 billion yearly profit in 2018, then went on to post a $5.83 billion loss in fiscal 2023 during the last downturn. Sales would go from over $30 billion in 2018 to $21 billion in 2020, then back up over $30 billion and back down again. Investors got burned many times, and Micron was often cheap for a reason. The stock seemed to live in the $40 range for years as I remember, but that narrative is history.

Today, it shows some real consistency in their recent numbers: Q1 revenue $8.71B, Q2 $8.05B, Q3 $9.30B, Q4 $11.32B. That is solid and displays some real growth. Looking ahead to next quarter, management is forecasting $1.2 billion in sequential revenue growth. Full-year operating cash flow reached $17.5 billion, up from $8.5 billion the previous year. This isn't the previous "uninvestable" volatility, this is the up and to the right I need to see in good investments. This is AI growth.

Growth of HBM

The growth Micron is seeing is not because dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) has grown in price or demand, although it has. The AI chips need specialized high-bandwidth memory capable of feeding data to the GPUs fast enough to keep those processors running at full throttle. Micron is one of only three companies in the world, that can make HBM at scale, the others being SK Hynix and Samsung, If you know these names, you're aware Micron has firmly been in third place for a while.

However, a recent report said that Micron had reached second place in the HBM category. Astute Group reports that SK hynix Inc. has 62% market share, followed by Micron at 21% and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) at 17%. That may change, but whether second or third, the amount of HBM Micron sells is growing fast.

HBM sales are growing and will continue as its newest HBM3E is now shipping to both NVIDIA and AMD. AMD's flagship AI platform, the Instinct MI350 series, uses Micron's HBM3E. It also supplies NVIDIA's Blackwell B200 and GB200 platforms. Micron supplies both dominant AI accelerator ecosystems, along with the previously mentioned other two memory makers. Beyond HBM, Micron is the sole supplier of LPDRAM for NVIDIA's Grace CPU, and if that continues they are likely to supply the next "Vera" Nvidia CPU coming next year.

On the other side of the product mix is NAND, the fast storage technology that was 26% of the revenues as recently as Q1 of this year (Q1 2025 earnings call). As of Q4, due to the extreme growth of HBM, NAND is now 20% of revenues, however it is growing as well. It recorded record revenue of $8.5 billion, which is 18% YoY growth. Behind the scenes is is a little mixed. The actual shipments were single digits lower, but pricing was single digits stronger.

The Growth Multiplier Effect

I’ll admit the Hopper, Blackwell, Blackwell Ultra is all a bit much, but GPU math is easy. As GPU generations advance, each chip doesn't need just a little more memory. It needs exponentially more.

Inside each NVIDIA H100 chip was 80GB of memory. The H200 jumped to 141GB. The newer B200 came with 192GB, while the B300 packs 288GB. That is a 50% increase just from B200 to B300, but a 260% increase from the H100 (Hopper) to B300 (Blackwell Ultra)in less than two years.

So from Hopper to Blackwell, the memory growth was more than 3x. The next generation after Blackwell will need even more memory. The nature of these chips creates a multiplier effect for memory makers. We're already seeing this in the financial results.

HBM is included in Micron’s Cloud Memory Business (CMBU). CMBU grew 257% YoY with Q4 revenue of $4.5 billion. This is also their highest margin unit, at 59% gross margins as seen in the business units breakdown below. Data center revenue more than doubled year over year. HBM revenue broke through the $1 billion quarterly mark in Q2 this year, and in Q4.

In fiscal Q4, our HBM revenue grew to nearly $2 billion, implying an annualized run rate of about $8 billion. We continue to be sold out of HBM capacity through calendar 2025, with strong pricing momentum into 2026.

Micron’s gross margin reached 45.7% last quarter, and they're guiding to over 51% this quarter. That is margin expansion in action, and it's sustainable because the product mix keeps shifting toward these premium products.

Manufacturing Growth

Micron is reducing its foreign manufacturing exposure by onshoring much of its future manufacturing. Back in 2023 an initial $15 billion Idaho fab broke ground and is scheduled for production in 2027. Then this summer a total $200 billion US investment was announced, with a New York facility as the focal point and expanding the Virginia and Idaho facilities, while bringing advanced HBM packaging to the United States. Government backing makes these investments and benefits even better. Micron secured $6.165 billion in CHIPS Act funding plus a 25% Investment Tax Credit for the Idaho, New York, and Virginia expansions.

The actual benefits of this onshoring aren’t quantifiable at this point. Micron has only said they plan to produce up to 40% of DRAM in the US. They have said this will drive economies of scale and improve time to market. However, the most important number, as is almost always is price. We do know that Micron can afford it. 2025 just finished and they had $3.7 billion in adjusted free cash flow, while spending $13.8 billion on CapEx. They're funding this massive manufacturing expansion with operating cash flow, and are still cash flow positive. As the new facilities come online from 2026-2029, that cash generation will only accelerate.

You can’t make a semiconductor investment without looking at the China numbers, and the bad news has already been priced in. Micron’s revenue splits 65% United States, 15% Taiwan, 7% China, with the rest spread across the world. In May 2023, China banned Micron products from "critical infrastructure," which worried investors. At only 7% of current revenue, Micron will not be the negating chess piece that some other semi’s might be. Micron actually recently announced that it will continue selling to Chinese customers with its Lenovo relationship recently confirmed.

Growth in Margins and Valuation

Micron's Cloud Memory Business Unit, which includes HBM, brought in $4.5 billion in Q4 revenue with 59% gross margins and 48% operating margins, compared to historical 20-30% gross margins in memory. That's not the same business anymore.

The rest of the business units are doing very well, too. All three other business units, Core data center, Mobile and Client, and Automotive and Embedded were all up, both in revenue and margins.

Micron trades like a bank stock at a forward P/E of around 11x and a PEG ratio of 0.5x. But it should be trading at tech innovator level. Just look at these headlines, all taken from their newsroom, I’ll just link to the newsroom, but there are probably even newer faster, lower power technology being announced by the time you get there, like Micron's LPCAMM2 Powering AI-Ready Laptops with 8,533MT/s Speeds.

Management expects 2026 to continue to be a healthy demand environment, saying, they expect both DRAM and NAND growth in 2026. Overall they expect the HBM market growing to 35B for 2026 and are forecasting 100B by 2030. Given the growth of CMBU, the gross and net margins will increase and normalize. Along with lower revenue volatility, I believe the market will upgrade Micron.

Currently, Seeking Alpha has a consensus 2026 EPS estimate of 16.68. At its current P/E of 26.8x, that would forecast a price of $447 at the end of next year.

P/E Multiple

The company generated more than $3.7 billion in adjusted free cash flow last year while investing almost $14 billion in new capacity. And as the new fabs come online from 2026-2029, that cash generation will only accelerate. Tech companies with huge gross margins, superior performance, giant moats, and multi-year demand don't trade at 11x earnings. They trade at 20-30x. That means on the low end, 20x next year's EPS estimates of around $17 puts a potential stock price over $334.

Risks

There are risks worth mentioning. HBM manufacturing is complex, and the New York plant has already faced delay after delay. Micron must scale production while meeting NVIDIA's quality standards. Production gets tougher as you stack more layers, and American manufacturing can be more expensive as other semiconductor companies have stated. The delays are disappointing but latest earnings call did provide solid progress on New York and Idaho. The Idaho facility has reached construction milestones and received the CHIPS disbursement for that achievement. It plans to have output beginning in the second half of 2027. In New York, pre-construction studies were completed and power has been set up at the construction site.

Additionally, competitors aren't going away. SK hynix has been the leader for years, and Samsung is pouring resources into catching up. Samsung has recently secured qualification with Nvidia to provide HBM3E, making that race for second place closer. However, Micron doesn’t actually need to be first, second or third. Their current generation of HBM is qualified for Nvidia and AMD and most importantly the HBM market is growing rapidly. As stated before, they are sold out. The only concern is how the next generation, HBM4, is developing. On the Q4 earnings call we got this piece of news from the CEO, “We believe Micron’s HBM4 outperforms all competing HBM4 products.” So I think competition is really not a concern.

Conclusion: The Most Undervalued AI Play

The growth story is solid. First, every new GPU generation needs exponentially more memory, creating more demand. As more companies adopt AI, demand for high margin memory will continue. Second, the higher HBM sales lead directly to higher growth margins. Third, the plan to grow capacity is underway. Companies with huge gross margins, growing revenues, and in the center of the decade's hottest trade don't usually trade at 11x forward earnings.

data.title: Oracle Stock And Its Real Value
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) is among the largest technology companies in the world, which deals with database software, enterprise applications, and fast-expanding services in the cloud infrastructure. It was established in 1977, and since then, it has developed as a pioneering database company to become a global leader in cloud computing, AI, and data management solutions. It also provides an assortment of enterprise resource planning (ERP) and customer relationship management (CRM) software and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), which is directly competing with larger market competitors such as Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud.

Oracle recently appointed Clay Magouyrk as the president of cloud infrastructure, and Mike Sicilia as the president of industries, both will work as co-CEOs. The new leadership brings new hopes in regard to its dedication to the company, to its high-growth cloud, and industry-specific software strategies. This shift is part of a bigger plan by Oracle to enhance its cloud architecture and AI projects in light of the heavy competition in the tech sector.

Access to Nvidia's (NVDA) powerful computer chips and a growing number of companies using its Gen2 cloud platform helped Oracle become one of the key leaders in the recent wave of AI. However, some experts say that Oracle’s success and high stock price rely massively on the $300 billion cloud deal with OpenAI, and that about $500 billion in future orders come from this partnership. It is deemed highly risky if OpenAI’s (OPENAI) expected revenue growth doesn’t go as planned.

Even with those risks, Oracle continues to expand its global footprint through strategic collaborations. Its recent move to work with SoftBank in Japan shows a major milestone by offering secure AI and cloud services using Oracle Alloy. This partnership will help Japanese organizations safely store and manage data within the country while using advanced AI tools. It shows Oracle’s goal to combine cloud tech, data protection, and AI as part of its long-term growth plan.

Snapshot of the Company

ORCL seems to have a good standing, with a company rating score of 77.74. However, despite this high score, its fundamentals reflect a mix of strengths and weaknesses, indicating uneven performance across different categories.

Fundamentals

In the chart below, we can see a mostly consistent increase in price per share over the last 10 years, with very minor dips. For instance, the price went down in 2023, but was immediately followed by a notable surge of 40% in the next year.

Overall, the share price average has grown by about 227.5% over the past 10 years, or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 14.09%. This return shows strong growth and that the company is expanding steadily.

Earnings

Looking at the earnings history of ORCL, we can see that the EPS has generally grown over the past 10 years, with some notable fluctuations in certain years. It went from 2.07 in 2016 to 4.34 in 2025, reflecting a long-term growth trend, despite a few dips, such as in 2022, where it went from 4.55 to 2.41.

This drop is more evident because of the spike in earnings in 2021, which was due to a one-time tax benefit of $2.3 billion. That happened when Oracle moved some of its intellectual property between different legal entities, which lowered its taxes. In 2022, this benefit didn’t occur, so EPS naturally appeared low.

Despite these dips, Oracle’s earnings growth has been driven by its cloud services, software licenses, and subscriptions. And all of these are conducive to recurring revenue streams of the company. There exists a high intensity of competition among Oracle and cloud computing companies, such as Amazon (AMZN) AWS, Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOGL), but due to its wide customer base in the enterprise market and constant innovations, Oracle has a stable base in terms of earnings growth.

When removing the one-time tax benefit year, the earnings growth chart would be smoother and would display a steadier growth pattern for Oracle. While Oracle does experience some volatility, which is common among high-growth tech stocks, it is assuring to know that Oracle has the ability to recover from short-term volatility.

Return on Equity

Oracle’s return on equity (ROE) has shown some extreme fluctuations over the past five years. We can see it was at 0% or unrecognized in 2022 and 2023, while it peaked at 214.11% in 2024. These swings could look confusing in the graph, but one reason is that in these years, there was a significant increase in capital expenditures to fuel expansion of its cloud infrastructure, making the ROE lower. However, when looking at the average 5-year ROE, we can see that the company shows an efficient use of equity.

For return on equity, I look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. Oracle’s 5-year average is at 152.75%, so it is far above typical expectations from a strong-performing company.

Let's compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of 333 Software (System & Application) companies is 27.66%.

Therefore, ORCL’s 5-year average of 152.75% is well above average, and so is the current ROE of 85.36%.

Return on Invested Capital

Oracle’s return on invested capital (ROIC) has also been highly volatile over the past five years. It is because of the same reason, as we discussed in the ROE section, the increase in capital expenditures resulted in a lower ROIC.

The company’s ROIC peaked in 2021 at 17.9% and despite the fluctuations, the five-year average stands at 15.23%, which is close to typical expectations of a healthy company. For return on invested capital, I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, Oracle is just slightly below this benchmark.

Gross Margin Percent

Oracle’s gross margin percent (GMP) has faced a gradual decline over the last five years, from being at 80.59% in 2021 to 70.51% in 2025. Despite the downward trend being concerning, we can see that the overall gross margin remains very strong, reflecting the company’s high profitability from software and cloud services.

The five-year average GMP is 74.89%, which is well above typical expectations. I typically look for companies with gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, Oracle demonstrates a strong ability to maintain excellent margins over time.

Financial Stability

Considering other fundamentals of the balance sheet, we see that Oracle’s debt-to-equity ratio is 4.36, which is very high. This indicates that the company relies more on debt than equity to finance its operations, which can increase financial risk.

Oracle's current ratio is 0.62; ideally, we’d want to see a Current Ratio of more than 1, so this suggests that short-term liquidity is tight, and the company may not have enough current assets to cover immediate liabilities.

Oracle also pays a small dividend yield of 0.71%, indicating that most profits are likely reinvested into the business rather than returned to shareholders. Overall, Oracle seems to carry high leverage and low short-term liquidity.

Value vs. Price

The 10-year and 5-year average PE Ratio of ORCL has typically been 29.2 and 33.8, respectively. This indicates that ORCL could be currently trading at a high price when comparing its current P/E ratio of 64.1 to its average historical P/E Ratio range of 29 to 34.

The Estimated Value of the Stock is $123.33, versus the current stock price of $275.15. This indicates that ORCL is currently not selling at a bargain price. In fact, the stock appears overvalued based on its estimated fair value.

The above analysis shows an average valuation of around $140 per share versus its current price of about $283, this would indicate that Oracle Corporation stock is overpriced.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

On the surface Oracle Corporation could be seen as a good investment because its share price and income have been steadily growing throughout the last ten years. The strong performance of the company in the cloud services and software subscriptions is prevailing, and the company’s revenues continue to grow.

The return on equity of Oracle is somewhat unusually good in certain years and very low in others, but this is primarily a result of variance in capital expenditures, not low profitability. Its returns continue to show that it is efficient in utilizing shareholders’ money. Return on invested capital is below the ideal benchmark, but it still shows that Oracle makes healthy profits from its investments.

Gross margins are also exceptional as a result of the high profitability of its cloud and software business, though it has gradually declined over the past years. The company’s major weakness is the high debt-to-equity ratio and low current ratio, which means that the company depends significantly on debt and has constrained liquidity in the short term.

ORCL shares seem to be overpriced based on valuation terms in comparison with its long-term average P/E ratio, intrinsic value, and detailed valuation analysis.

Valuation metrics indicate that Oracle is overpriced. Like many AI-related companies, the buying craze is pushing Oracle shares at a high premium. There seems to be a heavy belief in its future of cloud computing, AI integration, as well as its dominance in enterprise software.

Although the stock seems overpriced currently, the positive fundamentals of Oracle make it a company that is financially robust and strategically significant. The fact that its earnings have been increasing steadily and it has good gross margins shows that it can continue to be profitable. Nonetheless, its high debt/equity ratio and low current ratio indicate that the company is being run on low liquidity and high leverage levels, which are areas that investors must watch keenly.

In my case, this decision would be cautious optimism. I will add Oracle to my watch list as it satisfies the many of the essentials of a strong and well-managed company. However, I would not buy it at the present price because the valuation appears to be way above a bargain price. I will wait to see if there is a pullback that would bring the stock price back to a more reasonable level. It’s very possible that the stock could continue to go up as more investors invest in the AI craze. But as a value investor, I’m taking a more cautious approach.
